title: Increasing MeCP2 protein in Pitt-Hopkins syndrome model (Tcf4) mice does not affect abnormal myelination but induces the generation of astrocytes.
authors: Vermudez SAD, Freitas GA, Buch A, Moxley Y, Rajpal H, Gogliotti RG, Niswender CM
abstract: Pitt-Hopkins syndrome (PTHS) results from loss-of-function (LOF) mutations in TCF4 and resembles Rett syndrome (caused by MECP2). Increasing MeCP2 levels (genetic or viral vector) normalizes reciprocal behavioral phenotypes in Tcf4 and MECP2-overexpressing animals; behavioral rescue extends to contextual fear learning. Tcf4 mutant mice show oligodendrocyte arrest at an immature stage. RNA-seq found that increasing MeCP2 does not dramatically alter the Tcf4-heterozygous transcriptional profile, and OL gene expression/molecular phenotypes are unchanged in the presence of an MECP2 transgene. However, increased Olig2/Gfap co-expression and astrocytic-morphology cells appear in Tcf4 mice with the MECP2 transgene, suggesting MeCP2-TCF4 interplay in astrocyte development.
